---
title: "28: Statistics - Generalized Linear Models"
format:
  html:
    css: homework-styles.css
---

<script src="homework-scripts.js"></script>

# 📚 Նյութը

- [📺 GLMs: Exponential Family, Link Functions, Poisson Regression (ToDo)](), [🎞️ Սլայդեր](Lectures/stat/13_stat.pdf)
- [🛠️📺 Practical (ToDo)]()


---

# 🏡 Տնային


---

## 1) GLM Framework & Poisson Regression (Lecture 13)

### 01 ER Visits: Poisson GLM {data-difficulty="2"}
A hospital records the number of ER visits per day along with predictors: whether it is a weekend ($\text{weekend}_i \in \{0,1\}$), the daily temperature ($\text{temp}_i$ in °C), and a holiday indicator ($\text{holiday}_i \in \{0,1\}$).

- a) **Simulate 365 days of data:** Generate $Y_i \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda_i)$ where
$$\log(\lambda_i) = 3.5 + 0.3 \cdot \text{weekend}_i - 0.02 \cdot \text{temp}_i + 0.5 \cdot \text{holiday}_i.$$
Use weekend occurring with probability 2/7, temperature $\sim N(15, 8^2)$, and about 10 holidays per year.
- b) Fit a Poisson GLM using `statsmodels` (family `Poisson`, link `log`). Report the estimated coefficients, standard errors, $z$-statistics, and $p$-values. Do you recover the true coefficients?
- c) Interpret the "weekend" coefficient as a **rate ratio**: $e^{0.3} \approx 1.35$ means 35% more ER visits on weekends. Compute 95% CIs for all rate ratios $e^{\hat{\beta}_j}$.
- d) Check for **overdispersion**: compute the ratio of the Pearson $\chi^2$ statistic to the residual degrees of freedom. If this ratio exceeds 1.5, refit as a quasi-Poisson model and compare the standard errors. What changes?

### 02 Normal GLM = OLS: A Proof {data-difficulty="3"}
Show that ordinary least squares is a special case of the GLM framework.

- a) Write the Normal density $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ in **exponential family** form:
$$f(y \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = h(y, \sigma^2) \exp\!\left(\frac{\theta \cdot y - b(\theta)}{a(\phi)}\right).$$
Identify the natural parameter $\theta$, the log-partition function $b(\theta)$, the dispersion $\phi$, and verify that $b'(\theta) = \mu$ and $b''(\theta) = 1$.
- b) Show that the **canonical link** for the Normal is the identity function: $g(\mu) = \mu$, which gives the linear predictor $\mu_i = \mathbf{x}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}$.
- c) The GLM **deviance** is
$$D = 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n}\Big[\ell_i(\hat{\mu}_i^{\text{sat}}) - \ell_i(\hat{\mu}_i)\Big]$$
where $\hat{\mu}_i^{\text{sat}} = y_i$ (saturated model). Show that for the Normal GLM:
$$D = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{\mu}_i)^2 = \frac{\text{RSS}}{\sigma^2}.$$
- d) Conclude: OLS is the GLM with Normal distribution + identity link. The $F$-test, $t$-tests, $R^2$, and all standard regression results are special cases of GLM inference.

### 03 Insurance Claims: Gamma GLM {data-difficulty="2"}
Insurance claim amounts are positive and right-skewed — a natural fit for the Gamma distribution.

- a) **Simulate** $n = 500$ claims: $Y_i \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\text{shape} = 5, \text{rate} = 5/\mu_i)$ where
$$\log(\mu_i) = 6 + 0.5 \cdot \frac{\text{age}_i}{50} + 0.3 \cdot \text{male}_i.$$
Generate ages uniformly in $[20, 70]$ and gender as $\mathrm{Bernoulli}(0.5)$.
- b) Fit a **Gamma GLM** with log link using `statsmodels`. Report and interpret the coefficients as **multiplicative effects** on the mean claim amount. For instance, $e^{\hat{\beta}_{\text{male}}}$ gives the ratio of mean claims for males vs females.
- c) **Compare** with a log-transformed OLS model: regress $\log(Y)$ on the predictors using ordinary least squares. Are the coefficients similar? Why might they differ? *(Hint: $E[\log Y] \neq \log E[Y]$ — the log-OLS models the geometric mean, while the Gamma GLM models the arithmetic mean.)*
- d) Create a QQ-plot of the **deviance residuals**. Does the Gamma assumption hold? How do the deviance residuals compare to the raw residuals?

### 04 Unifying Exercise: OLS vs Logistic vs Poisson {data-difficulty="2"}
Demonstrate that GLMs unify different types of regression under one framework.

- a) Create three response variables from the same set of predictors $X_1, X_2$: (i) a **continuous** outcome $Y_{\text{cont}} = 2 + X_1 - 0.5X_2 + \varepsilon$, (ii) a **binary** outcome $Y_{\text{bin}} = \mathbf{1}\{Y_{\text{cont}} > 2\}$, and (iii) a **count** outcome $Y_{\text{count}} \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(e^{0.5 + 0.3X_1 - 0.1X_2})$.
- b) Fit three GLMs: Normal + identity link, Binomial + logit link, Poisson + log link. For each, report the coefficient table.
- c) For each model, interpret the coefficient of the most important predictor in the **natural scale**: mean difference (OLS), odds ratio (logistic), rate ratio (Poisson).
- d) Create a summary table comparing the three GLMs:

| Property | OLS | Logistic | Poisson |
|---|---|---|---|
| Distribution | Normal | Bernoulli | Poisson |
| Link function | ? | ? | ? |
| Effect measure | ? | ? | ? |
| Test statistic | ? | ? | ? |

### 05 Deviance and Model Comparison via LRT {data-difficulty="2"}
Use the Poisson GLM from Problem 01.

- a) Fit a **reduced model** that drops the "holiday" predictor. Report its deviance.
- b) Compute the difference in deviances $\Delta D = D_{\text{reduced}} - D_{\text{full}}$. Under $H_0$ (holiday has no effect), $\Delta D \sim \chi^2_1$ by Wilks' theorem. Compute the $p$-value.
- c) Compare with **AIC** ($\text{AIC} = D + 2p$ where $p$ is the number of parameters). Do the LRT and AIC agree on whether to include "holiday"?
- d) Now fit an **overly complex model** by adding day-of-year as a continuous predictor. Does the LRT say it is significant? Does AIC prefer it? Discuss the connection to overfitting.

### 06 Negative Binomial: Handling Overdispersion {data-difficulty="2"}

- a) **Simulate** overdispersed count data: $Y_i \sim \mathrm{NegBin}(r = 3, p_i)$ where $\log(\mu_i) = 2 + 0.5 X_i$ and $X_i \sim N(0, 1)$. *(Use the mean-dispersion parameterization: $\mu = r(1-p)/p$, $\text{Var}(Y) = \mu + \mu^2/r$.)*
- b) Fit a **Poisson GLM**. Compute the overdispersion diagnostic: Pearson $\chi^2$ divided by the residual degrees of freedom. Why does the Poisson model underestimate the standard errors when there is overdispersion?
- c) Fit a **Negative Binomial GLM** (using `statsmodels`). Compare the standard errors with the Poisson model. Which are larger? By what factor?
- d) Show that as $r \to \infty$, the Negative Binomial converges to a Poisson. *(Hint: write $\text{Var}(Y) = \mu + \mu^2/r$. As $r \to \infty$, $\text{Var}(Y) \to \mu$, which is exactly the Poisson variance.)*

### 07 Link Function Misspecification {data-difficulty="3"}

- a) Generate binary data using a **probit** model: $P(Y = 1 \mid X) = \Phi(\beta_0 + \beta_1 X)$ where $\Phi$ is the standard normal CDF, $\beta_0 = 0$, $\beta_1 = 1$, and $X \sim N(0, 1)$ with $n = 1000$.
- b) Fit both a **logistic** regression (logit link) and a **probit** regression to the same data. Compare the estimated coefficients. Verify the well-known scaling relationship: $\hat{\beta}_{\text{logit}} \approx 1.7 \cdot \hat{\beta}_{\text{probit}}$.
- c) Plot the predicted probabilities $\hat{P}(Y = 1 \mid X)$ from both models across $X \in [-3, 3]$. How different are the predictions in the middle of the data range ($|X| < 1$)? At the extremes ($|X| > 2$)?
- d) When does the choice of link function matter most? *(The logistic and normal CDFs are very similar in shape; the logistic has slightly heavier tails. Differences are most pronounced for extreme probabilities near 0 or 1.)*


# 🎲 38 (01) TODO
- ▶️[ToDo]()
- 🔗[Random link]()
- 🇦🇲🎶[ToDo]()
- 🌐🎶[ToDo]()
- 🤌[Կարգին]()


<a href="http://s01.flagcounter.com/more/1oO"><img src="https://s01.flagcounter.com/count2/1oO/bg_FFFFFF/txt_000000/border_CCCCCC/columns_2/maxflags_10/viewers_0/labels_0/pageviews_1/flags_0/percent_0/" alt="Flag Counter"></a>
